relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Job sees human life as hard service under the sun.
Like a servant longing for shade, he longs for relief.
Months feel empty, nights assigned to trouble.
Sleep does not bring rest; the night stretches and tosses him.
His body itself has become a field of pain and dust.
His days fly faster than a shuttle, ending without hope.
He asks the Divine to remember that his life is breath.
Soon the one who sees him will no longer find him.
Like a vanishing cloud, the one who descends to Sheol does not rise back into ordinary life.
House and place will no longer know him.
So he refuses silence and speaks from bitter soul-distress.
He asks why he is guarded as if he were the Sea or the Monster.
Even his bed cannot comfort him.
Dreams and visions terrify instead of heal.
His soul longs for release from the body of pain.
He asks to be left alone because his days are only breath.
He turns Psalm-like wonder into protest: what is a human that you pay so much attention?
He experiences divine attention as constant testing.
He begs for even a moment without being watched.
He asks what his sin could do to the watcher of humanity.
He asks why guilt is not lifted before he returns to dust.
